Name: latency_aware_layer_skipping
Title: Latency-Aware Layer-Skipping via Jacobian-Based Perturbation Analysis and PAC-Bayes Spectral Bounds
Short Hypothesis: A training-free, Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework can minimize hidden-state perturbation while strictly enforcing latency budgets, achieving accuracy-latency Pareto efficiency.
Related Work: Existing works like HiLoRA and AMP-ViT focus on domain generalization and quantization but do not explicitly address latency-aware layer-skipping or hidden-state perturbation minimization. This proposal uniquely combines PAC-Bayes spectral bounds with Jacobian-based perturbation analysis to enforce strict latency constraints.
Abstract: Large models often face significant inference latency, especially in resource-constrained environments. Existing methods for latency reduction, such as quantization or pruning, often require retraining or fail to strictly enforce latency budgets. We propose a novel training-free framework for latency-aware layer-skipping that leverages Jacobian-based perturbation analysis and PAC-Bayes spectral bounds. Our approach minimizes hidden-state perturbation while strictly adhering to latency constraints, ensuring computational tractability and robustness. Experiments on large language models demonstrate that our framework achieves superior accuracy-latency Pareto efficiency compared to existing methods, with minimal overhead.
Experiments: Latency-Accuracy Pareto Frontiers: Evaluate the trade-off between accuracy and latency on large language models (e.g., GPT-3, LLaMA) using our framework versus baselines (e.g., AMP-ViT, HiLoRA).
Perturbation-Cost Analysis: Measure hidden-state perturbation (via Frobenius norm of Jacobian) and its impact on model accuracy under varying latency budgets.
Wall-Clock Budget Enforcement: Validate strict adherence to latency budgets across different hardware setups (e.g., single GPU, multi-GPU).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk: Jacobian computation may introduce overhead; mitigate via efficient approximations.
Limitation: Framework assumes access to pre-trained models; applicability to training-from-scratch scenarios is unexplored.
Risk: Spectral bounds may be too conservative; refine via empirical calibration.
